data.title: Pampa Energía: No New Catalysts, But Potential In Prices
data.description:
Since I wrote my last article about Pampa Energía (PAM) on January 12, the stock has performed broadly in line with the S&P 500 (SPY). My rating was Hold because I thought the market was already pricing in its long-term potential. I still believe its shares are priced at an entry premium, but now I believe it's time to buy before the stock breaks its all-time highs again. I see Pampa as a great opportunity to participate in Argentina's energy sector boom.

In the mentioned article, I explained the company's operations and its strength in gas and oil as well as in electricity generation. But I focused more on explaining the weight of its sales based on crude oil combined with an analysis of the strong oil inventory I was seeing at the beginning of 2026.

This led me to some estimates that were quite wrong regarding the possible price of crude oil. These were not random estimates. I based my thesis on EIA reports that predicted an average price for Q1 2026 of $50-$59. The conflict in the Middle East changed the context of the energy markets, and now the expected average price is $96, based on their latest report. The estimated average price in March was $79, a 22% difference, which shows the current uncertainty of markets. Meanwhile, I also checked future crude oil prices according to CME Group, and in Q4 2026 it drops to less than $75.

However, my analysis was thought out for the long term. I mean, in the short term, I guess I was wrong. But in the long term, I believe that oversupply and overstocking will continue to exist. In the end, my long-term thesis didn't change, although a big part of the energy infrastructure in the Middle East was damaged. What I'm trying to say is, ultimately, the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz wasn't just a logistical problem, but the damaged infrastructure could raise crude oil prices somewhat in the long term compared to prewar estimates. But the structural situation of long-term reserves is comfortable for the world economy.

Following the EIA reports, the base scenario projects a recovery of the surplus for the third quarter of the year, and 2026 would end up with a total increase in average supply of 300 kb/d. In the case of a more difficult scenario, the deficit at the end of the year would be approximately 4 to 5 mb/d (chart at the left). That would be, for me, the worst-case scenario, and it would cause prices to remain much closer to $100 than $70 for a much longer period. However, I believe that reserves remain strong enough to maintain the supply of crude oil intact. And although the infrastructure and distribution suffered serious damage, I think the balance against demand would come out more than well in the base scenario.

The Weight of Crude Oil in Pampa: Why Didn't the Stock Rise With the Supply Cuts?

Beyond the analysis of the crude oil market, I believe the important thing is to understand that Pampa's role remains key in Argentina's energy sector. In my last article, I told you I was very excited for Pampa's investments in Vaca Muerta and calculated that a crude oil price below $55-$60 could put pressure on the company's profitability in the medium term. Now, those prices are very far away due to the conflict in the Middle East.

However, Pampa's shares did not experience an extraordinary rise as a result of the increase in oil prices, which surpassed $100 on several occasions. For example, in companies operating in Vaca Muerta, such as Vista Energy (VIST), the stock rose 33% in 2026. Pampa, on the other hand, has fallen 8% so far in 2026.

I believe this is due, in part, to the fact that VIST has oil at the core of its business, while Pampa has more diversified income, with more weight in gas production and electricity generation. That's what I found in the annual reports. Pampa had sales of $2 billion in 2025 (slide 63), and Oil & Gas accounted for $740 million. On the other hand, VIST had revenues of $2.47 billion, of which $2.38 billion was for crude oil sales.

As I explained in my previous article, Pampa chose approximately three years ago to get into the business of pure crude oil with the purchase of the Rincón de Aranda field, located in the Vaca Muerta formation and wholly owned by the company. This move positioned Pampa as a competitor to Argentina's major oil producers, such as YPF and VIST. But Pampa's core was (and still is) gas.

The company's total oil production is 81,200 barrels of oil equivalent per day (kboepd), of which only 18,000 kboepd are sold as crude oil, approximately 23%. On the other hand, the rest of the production belongs to 11 million cubic meters per day. Translated into crude oil measurements, this equates to between 64 and 65 kboepd. To sum up: 77% of Pampa's oil production is used for gas production.

This is also something I saw in the four oil and gas blocks controlled by the company. Only Rincón de Aranda is a pure oil block, while the others (Sierra Chata, Río Neuquén, and El Mangrullo) have gas infrastructure. In terms of profitability, I clearly see pure oil production is giving a really interesting EBITDA. From $375 million EBITDA in E&P (Exploration and Production), $126 million corresponds to oil.

However, when I incorporated this operating segment into the total, the oil margins were lower than I had anticipated. In summary, E&P represented 35% of the Adjusted EBITDA in 2025, but the weight of gas is bigger, so pure oil EBITDA is between 11% and 13% of the total. The rest of the crude market corresponds to gas distribution, which is handled by Transportadora de Gas del Sur S.A. (TGS), a company co-controlled by Pampa. TGS is a key part of Pampa's midstream business, which only underscores the gas focus. The other half of Pampa's margins comes from electricity generation. 44% of Adjusted EBITDA comes from generation (Slide 7 of the Investor Presentation).

Although the pure crude oil business of Pampa has very good margins, it still represents a small portion of total EBITDA. Meanwhile, the company accounts for it within the Oil & Gas segment. In 2025, this segment represented a $91 million EBIT, a better result compared to 2024, which was $61 million. However, EBIT was $503 million, so Oil & Gas represented 18%. The largest part of the EBIT increase came from electricity generation, which rose from $204 million to $375 million. In summary, electricity generation represented almost 75% of EBIT in 2025. So, electric generation seems to be the core of Pampa, generating higher sales revenue, higher EBIT, and the best EBITDA. The Utilities segment, managed by TGS and Transener, represents 15% of EBITDA.

Electricity Generation is the Core of Pampa, but Not Its Main Catalyst

The issue with electricity generation is that I believe it lacks strong long-term catalysts beyond natural demand growth and the deregulation of certain sectors. Even more: 79% of EBITDA comes from the regulated segment managed by the state-owned company CAMMESA.

If CAMMESA reduces its participation in the Argentine market as a central regulator, PAMPA could achieve better clients at better prices. The tariff catalysts, given that the electricity sector is highly regulated, were already priced in Pampa since Javier Milei's arrival to the presidency of Argentina in 2023.

The free market reforms made by the Argentine government to raise service prices caused companies like Pampa, whose business is largely focused on the electricity market, to benefit. In the last three years, Pampa's shares rose almost 140%. At the same time, Argentina's macroeconomic stability boosted the company's investments, which totaled $1.2 billion in 2025. But of that CAPEX, $1 billion was dedicated to oil and gas.

So, what I see in the generation sector of Pampa, the more stable sector from my point of view and with the greatest strength to generate cash flow, is that today it lacks clear catalysts. With the exception of privatizations or further deregulation measures in the electricity market. Another catalyst could be increasing the market share in generation, which is currently 15%, but I believe these possibilities are already priced into the market.

Outlook for Q1 2026 Earnings

Given this outlook for the 2025 results, which I consider generally positive, Pampa will present its Q1 2026 earnings on May 6th. I believe it is very important to look carefully at the income of the Oil & Gas segment, and therefore I also believe we need to understand the data I showed you above.

I took a closer look at crude oil production. In 2025, daily barrel production rose 145%, while the average international barrel price went from $70 in 2024 to $61 in 2025, a 12% decrease. The production increase, based at the Rincón de Aranda field, allowed Pampa to achieve sales of $862 million, 18% higher than in 2024. But as I mentioned before, the segment's EBIT was only $91 million. Even more: the segment reported a total loss of $55 million due to financing costs.

My first impression of the oil environment is that Pampa could report a strong increase in revenue in Oil & Gas. The average WTI price in Q1 2026 was $71, representing an increase of nearly 18% compared to the $60 average recorded in the previous quarter. Although this increase may not represent the bulk of Oil & Gas revenue, as explained above, it will imply better reported margins and could point to a more positive outlook for 2026.

The market expects revenue of $473 million for the quarter, below the $504 million reported in the previous quarter. However, if this estimate is met, it would represent an increase of approximately 21% YoY. I also expect to see more details on expansion plans in the oil sector in the context of the conflict in the Middle East, supply cuts, and price volatility. As a guide, the company already outlined a production plan for 2029 in its 2025 annual report.

Valuation

I believe the stock price has long-term upside potential thanks to investments in Rincón de Aranda. The goal is to reach 45,000 barrels of oil per day in 2027, increasingly gaining ground in the crude oil business and capitalizing on the Vaca Muerta boom. The other segments of Pampa also excite me, and it's a stock I would hold for a long time.

However, as in my January article, the current price forces us to pay a premium for the company's potential. The EV/EBITDA multiple of 8x remains relatively in line with the 5-year average (8.7x). At the same time, the stock is trading at 7.7 times its expected earnings, while the 5-year average is also 7.5 times expected earnings.

But as I believe Pampa is still undervalued (I mean, its multiples could be even higher), I think it's time to upgrade my rating from Hold to Buy. Analyst estimates indicate an average target price of $112, which would imply a 40% increase. The lowest estimate is a target price of $90, an increase of approximately 20%. In other words, even in a more challenging environment, the company's weak performance so far in 2026, I believe, gives us a buying signal.

I don't see new catalysts beyond the ones that I analyzed before. But I do see the stock has been left behind compared to other energy companies. So, for anyone who has Pampa in their portfolio, I think it's time to increase their position.

Risks: Weak Momentum in the Argentine Market

In my previous investment thesis on Pampa, I discussed the risks of the Argentine market, its political uncertainty, and its economic volatility. I also mentioned the economic reforms that needed to be passed in Congress to make the labor market more flexible and offer tax incentives for investment, among other issues.

The main economic debates resulted in a victory for the ruling party. The labor reform suffered a minor legal setback, but the Argentine government expects to overcome it in the short term. So, to my surprise, news of the reforms' approval failed to boost Argentine equities, keeping the market almost at the same levels as after Milei's electoral victory in October 2025. I'm using the Global X MSCI Argentina ETF (ARGT) to illustrate this point.

From last October until now, the Argentine market has remained at an average price of $93-$94. This weak momentum in Argentine equities in 2026 affects all stocks in the country, including Pampa.

Frankly, I cannot explain with much certainty why all of this is happening. Analysts say that credit concerns and inflation, the persistence of capital controls (currency restrictions), and the shift of funds from equities to fixed income (sovereign bonds) are some of the determining factors behind the loss of momentum. Personally, I believe that MSCI's standalone rating remains the main obstacle for the Argentine market, limiting capital inflows. Despite all of this, the Argentine electricity sector is still favored by major firms, such as Morgan Stanley.

Conclusions

I believe Pampa Energía is a very attractive option to participate in the electric power development of Argentina, despite the political and economic challenges. I also highlighted the natural risk of making an investment in foreign markets. Argentina is not going through its best moment in its benchmark ETF that I used, ARGT.

I also couldn't find new catalysts for the company. In my previous article I also mentioned the main reasons to trust the company, and I recommended a Hold rating because the stock was trading at prices that already reflected its potential. Now, three months later and very close to the earnings presentation, I am upgrading my rating to Buy. Given that the company is achieving the best margins in the crude oil segment, in the end, the rise in oil prices could benefit Rincón de Aranda's profitability in the short term.

Given its leading position in the country's electricity generation market, I believe Pampa now deserves a chance, with analysts forecasting target prices near $90, even at the lower end of the range. So, I upgrade my rating on Pampa from Hold to Buy.

data.title: Riverwater Sustainable Value Strategy Q1 2026 Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Riverwater Sustainable Value Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary.

The Sustainable Value Strategy underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Value Index, in the first quarter.

Strategy Additions & Sales

New Positions

During the quarter, we initiated five positions: Leggett & Platt, Inc., Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc., Methanex, Dick's Sporting Goods, and Jack Henry & Associates.

Leggett & Platt, Inc. (LEG) is a diversified manufacturer of engineered components for bedding, furniture, and automotive markets. We'd wager that at least one bed in your home contains Leggett components — at 50% market share, the odds are squarely in our favor.

With a veteran CEO returning, the company recently completed a major restructuring expected to generate meaningful annual cost savings, while deleveraging through asset sales and a reduced dividend. The stock has sold off to multi-decade lows as cyclical weakness across its end markets has pressured earnings.

LEG trades at less than 10x 2026 estimated earnings vs. historically trading at over 17x. We are confident in the underlying business given its durable market leadership, vertically integrated supply chain, strong free cash flow generation (it trades at a double-digit free cash flow yield), and potential positive turn in the mattress industry. Furthermore, the recent unsolicited takeover bid from Somnigroup underscores the significant value not reflected in the current stock price.

Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (SFM) is a specialty grocery retailer focused on fresh, natural, and organic foods. Its unique health-focused stores built around its farm-stand heritage differentiate it from conventional grocers and position it to capitalize on the secular consumer shift toward better-for-you food. With stores in 25 states nationwide, the company is executing a long-term ~10% store growth algorithm, including into underpenetrated markets across the Northeast and Midwest.

The stock retreated meaningfully from its 2025 peak as comparable store sales decelerated due to macro-driven consumer caution. We are confident in the underlying business given its best-in-class gross margins, curated product selection driving customer loyalty, and improving fundamentals through private label expansion and supply chain self-distribution. We like that it trades at a below-market multiple of 13.9x 2026 estimated earnings, but still is on track to continue its double-digit earnings growth algorithm.

Methanex (MEOH) is the world's largest producer and distributor of methanol. We believe the stock is well positioned to benefit from a tightening global supply-demand backdrop, particularly in light of escalating conflict in the Middle East. The company's global production footprint and integrated logistics network provide a structural advantage in supplying customers during periods of disruption.

Recent geopolitical developments—specifically reduced Iranian supply and broader regional instability—are already contributing to higher methanol pricing. Importantly, this dynamic is occurring against a backdrop of structurally constrained supply and steady demand growth across industrial and energy-related applications, suggesting pricing strength may be more durable than typical cyclical spikes. It trades at 10.7x estimated 2026 earnings.

From a responsible business perspective, we believe that Methanex stands out within the chemicals sector. It has industry-leading safety performance, no major environmental incidents in recent years, and meaningful progress on emissions intensity reduction ahead of stated targets. The company is also actively investing in lower-carbon methanol pathways, including biomethanol and carbon capture initiatives, positioning it to participate in longer-term decarbonization trends such as marine fuel transition.

Dick's Sporting Goods (DKS) presents what we see to be a compelling investment case driven by a combination of cyclical tailwinds and company-specific execution. On the demand side, higher tax refunds relative to last year should provide incremental support to discretionary spending, particularly in categories such as athletic apparel, footwear, and equipment, where DKS has strong market positioning. This is further amplified by the upcoming 2026 FIFA World Cup in the United States, which is expected to drive increased participation and consumer interest in soccer and related sporting categories, creating a multi-quarter demand tailwind. It trades at 14.9x estimated 2026 earnings.

Importantly, DKS is also well positioned to benefit from improving industry dynamics, including a potential turnaround at Foot Locker in the second half of 2026. This could help stabilize key vendor relationships—most notably with Nike—and lead to a more rational promotional environment across athletic retail. While near-term consumer headwinds remain, we believe DKS's differentiated merchandising strategy, premium store experience, and strong relationship with youth sports position the company to capture outsized share as conditions normalize, supporting continued margin resilience and long-term earnings growth.

Jack Henry & Associates (JKHY) represents what we consider to be a high-quality compounder that we find to be recently more attractive following a valuation reset driven by broad-based concerns around AI disruption in financial technology. JKHY provides backend technology for small and medium sized banks. We believe the sell-off has been largely indiscriminate, creating a rare opportunity to acquire a mission-critical, deeply entrenched business at a meaningful discount to its historical valuation. We also believe their largest competitor will force RFPs to Jack Henry as they are consolidating platforms which can cause havoc for customers. This could create a very compelling opportunity to take market share.

JKHY's competitive moat remains intact, underpinned by several durable advantages: a highly regulated end market, long-standing customer relationships with community and regional banks, and deep integration within clients' core processing and digital infrastructure. These systems are not easily displaced, given high switching costs, regulatory complexity, and the operational risk associated with transitioning core banking platforms.

While the market has questioned the long-term impact of AI on traditional fintech providers, we view JKHY as a beneficiary rather than a casualty, given its role as a trusted technology partner and data steward within the banking ecosystem. With strong recurring revenue, high retention rates, and consistent free cash flow generation, JKHY offers a compelling combination of resilience and long-term growth, particularly at current valuation levels which we believe understate the durability of its business model.

Exits

We exited several positions during the quarter, reflecting our ongoing discipline around valuation, portfolio fit, and risk management.

We sold our position in Amdocs (DOX) as we chose to redeploy capital toward names with more attractive upside.

Our exit from Ciena (CIEN) came early in the quarter. The market cap blew past $50 billion (it is now $66 billion) and was added to the S&P 500. Ideally every company we sell would be for this reason.

Federal Agricultural Mortgage (AGM) was sold in several tranches through the quarter. The agricultural lending environment has remained stable, but we felt there is more upside in JKHY.

We closed our position in Lazard (LAZ). With the M&A cycle showing potential signs of slowing and two record-breaking IPOs stealing share, we felt the stock was pricing in a more optimistic capital markets environment than we were prepared to underwrite.

Our position in Western Alliance Bancorp (WAL) was liquidated in early March. The bank had been a strong holding for us, but we became nervous with their large loan balances to non depository financial institutions and credit issues with First Brands.

Disclosures: The information contained herein represents the opinion of Riverwater and should not be construed as personalized or individualized investment advice. Analysis and opinion expressed in this report are subject to change without notice. Reader should not assume that investments in the securities identified were or will be profitable. Timing differences of purchases and sales may have a modest impact on the actual contribution numbers presented. The holdings identified do not represent all the securities purchased, sold, or recommended. The calculation's methodology along with details on all holding's contribution to the overall account's performance during the measurement period are available upon request. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

The contributions presented are shown on a gross basis and do not reflect the deduction of management fees, or other fees. The deduction of management fees reduces investment returns and would also affect the investment characteristics presented. Each contributor's share of performance is shown for informational purposes only and is not predictive of future performance or investment outcomes.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Coherent: A Buy Driven By Robust AI Datacenter Optics Demand
data.description:
Coherent (COHR) is positioned at the intersection of three technology pillars underpinning the AI infrastructure buildout—compound semiconductors, photonics, and optical networking. The company was able to execute a sharp turnaround following a difficult downcycle in FY2023-FY2024, which saw challenges integrating after the II-VI/Coherent merger and inventory corrections. Revenue has rebounded back to $5.8B in FY2025 and is expected to reach $6.95B/$8.78B in FY2026E/FY2027E with growth rates of 19.6%/26.3% Y/Y, respectively. Critically, EPS is expected to compound at an even faster rate at a 3-year CAGR of ~45% from FY2025 to FY2028E.

The stock trades at an FY2027E P/E of 44.5x and a PEG ratio of 1.45x, which is not too expensive in the current market and its growth rate. It also boasts a defensible moat in its proprietary manufacturing of indium phosphide (InP) lasers and its emerging 1.6T/co-packaged optics franchise.

Investment thesis

1. The core engine for AI data center optics

AI hyperscalers deploy GPU clusters that need dense, high-bandwidth optical interconnections between each compute node at scale. Coherent has been a leading independent supplier of INP-based transceivers for 400G, 800G, and now 1.6T applications. As such, the management has reported record bookings that extend well into FY2027E with an impressive 4x book-to-bill for 1.6T.

The transition to 6-inch InP wafers has become the key supply-side differentiator. This technology delivers four times the chip count at less than half of the cost of the legacy 3-inch process. The production ramp began in August 2025, and Coherent targets a doubling of total InP capacity within FY2026E. This creates a structural cost wedge against competitors. At OFC 2026, the company demonstrated its 3.2T and 12.8T transceiver architectures, reassuring the longevity and visibility of Coherent's technological roadmap, which extends well beyond this cycle.

The market expects FY2026E to grow 19.6% Y/Y to $6.95B, driven primarily by the data center & communications segment. Revenue is projected to further accelerate to $8.78B in FY2027E, reflecting a 26.3% Y/Y increase. This is already partially supported by its record bookings.

2. Structural buildup in operating leverage

The three forces driving margin expansion are due to 1) the 6-inch InP wafer transition reducing unit manufacturing cost, 2) the mix shifting towards higher speed/ASP 800G and 1.6T transceivers that carry higher margins than legacy 100G/400G, and 3) a relatively fixed SG&A and R&D cost base. According to the Q2 FY2026 earnings call, CFO Sherri Luther has suggested a long-term adjusted gross margin that will exceed 42%, while market consensus estimates for the outer years lean more conservatively. Still, the trajectory is clear as margins have bottomed out in FY2024 and are moving in a positive direction.

Fiscal Year Adj. Gross Margin Adj. EBITDA Margin Adj. Net Income Margin
FY2024A 36.0% 21.3% 5.5%
FY2025A 37.9% 22.2% 9.7%
FY2026E 39.0% 23.6% 15.0%
FY2027E 40.4% 25.3% 17.4%
FY2028E 40.5% 28.6% 20.0%

Note: Coherent excludes 1) amortization of acquired tangibles, 2) stock-based compensation, and 3) integration/site consolidation charges in adjusted figures.

Source: Company, Market estimates, Himalayas Research estimates

3. Optionality in Silicon Carbide, yet de-risked

Reaching beyond optics, Coherent is a leading SiC substrate manufacturer for EV power inverters and AI data center power conversion systems. Denso and Mitsubishi Electric invested $1B in Coherent late 2023, which serves to validate the company's technology while also providing capital. A 300mm SiC platform was recently announced and will target AI data center thermal management in addition to EV and industrial electrification applications.

However, SiC revenue has been weak, with the Materials segment registering a 6% Y/Y drop in FY2025. We understand that this is a timing mismatch in EV ramp schedules rather than underlying softness. We believe that the market is discounting the optionality with the EV growth thesis, and because the Denso/Mitsubishi relationship provides a durable cushion, there could be asymmetric upside should EV adoption accelerates.

4. Emerging revenue streams from CPO, OCS, and Apple

Coherent has secured a large CPO (co-packaged optics) purchase, which will use its proprietary high-power CW lasers. The CPO architecture is expected to dominate next-generation AI switch designs at 3.2T and beyond and will replace pluggable modules with directly integrated laser sources to eliminate insertion loss.

The company is shipping optical circuit switch (OCS) systems to several customers. The management has doubled its prediction for its SAM from $2B to $4B (investor deck, pp. 21), fueled by broader use cases across scaling and spine applications, and faster-than-expected customer adoption.

Apple has entered into a multi-year laser supply agreement with the company and is expected to begin contributing revenue in H2 CY2026. This adds a diversified, first tier consumer electronics revenue stream to an otherwise hyperscaler-focused customer mix.

Balance sheet and capital expenditure

The company carries an improved debt-to-equity ratio of 0.41x with a net debt of $2.68B, having reduced total debt from $4.5B in FY2023 to $3.5B in its latest filing.

FCF was modest in FY2025 at $193M due to an elevated capex of $441M to fund the 6-inch InP and SiC capacity buildout. We expect FCF to accelerate during FY2027E to FY2028E as capex moderates and operating leverage improves. Market estimates put FY2027E FCF at $851M. Since the II-VI merger, management has opted for a more capital-light approach and remains focused on deleveraging.

Key Risks

Coherent has a high concentration of hyperscaler customers, so it could be subject to their capex cyclicality. So far, the AI capex cycle has bucked softer macro trends and remains robust.

There is intense competition from Lumentum (LITE), Marvell's (MRVL) potential in-house optics, and new entrants like POET, which will compete on transceiver pricing. Also, Nvidia could also vertically integrate into optics, creating a tail risk.

Automotive SiC demand is still below satisfaction, as we can see from the softness in the Materials segment. The timing with SiC lies on EV adoption, but is partially offset by partnership capital.

The company faces execution risks as it juggles scaling 6-inch InP, and 300mm SiC and building out CPO/OCS product lines.

Valuation and verdict

Coherent is a very compelling photonics ramp investment story. There is significant tailwind coming from non-discretionary optical infrastructure requirements of AI scaling. Revenue is expected to surge from $5.8B to $10.6B from FY2025 to FY2028E, or a CAGR of 22%, while EPS is set to grow even faster at a CAGR of 45%, thanks to expanding margins.

The stock is not trading too rich at ~45x FY27E P/E and an FY26E PEG ratio of 1.4x, especially if we consider the fast, compounding growth to be expected. The company has an InP manufacturing moat that no other pure-play photonics peer can replicate at scale.

We rate COHR a Buy.

FY2026E $6,950M $1,640M $5.42
FY2027E $8,776M $2,218M $7.81
FY2028E $10,642M $3,039M $10.80

Source: Market estimates, Himalayas Research estimates

FY2026E 64.1x 35.1x
FY2027E 44.5x 25.9x
FY2028E 32.2x 18.9x

data.title: Century Aluminum: A Decade-Long Growth Runway
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Riverwater Sustainable Value Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary.

Company Deep Dive: Century Aluminum Company (CENX)

CENX is the largest domestic producer of primary aluminum in the United States, operating smelters in Kentucky, South Carolina, and Iceland with combined annual production capacity of approximately 770,000 tonnes. Glencore plc is the largest shareholder at approximately 30% beneficial ownership 1 and also the company's largest customer (54% of 2025 sales). 2

U.S. Primary Aluminum

The U.S. went from producing 4.65 million tonnes of primary aluminum at 33 smelters in 1980 to approximately 670,000 tonnes at just 4 operating smelters today — a collapse from 33% of global output to under 1%. 3 The country now relies on imports for roughly 60% of its aluminum consumption. 4 Section 232 tariffs (currently 50% on imports) create a meaningful domestic price premium for U.S. producers. LME aluminum prices have risen above $3,300/ton 5 on tightening global supply, with Chinese production capped at 45 million tonnes and Gulf smelter capacity at risk from geopolitical disruption in the Strait of Hormuz — a dynamic we have termed the "Fog of Excursion" thesis.

Oklahoma Mega-Project

In January 2026, Century announced a landmark joint development agreement with Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) to build a 750,000-tonne-per-year primary aluminum smelter in Inola, Oklahoma — the first new U.S. smelter since 1980. Century will own 40% of the joint venture, supported by a $500 million DOE grant. 6 This single project would more than double current U.S. primary aluminum production and represents a decade-long growth runway. Construction is expected to begin late 2026, with first production by the end of the decade. 7

Financial Outlook: Century reported full-year 2025 adjusted EBITDA of $425.1 million on $2.53 billion of net sales. 8 Management guided Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $215–235 million, implying an annualized run rate approaching $900 million as higher LME pricing flows through on a lagged basis. 9 Near-term catalysts include the Mt. Holly capacity restart (50,000+ tonnes, targeting full production by June 2026), 10 Grundartangi Line 2 recovery to near-full output by July 2026, 11 and the Hawesville smelter monetization ($200 million cash plus a 6.8% equity stake in a TeraWulf-developed data center project). 12 Each $100/ton movement in LME aluminum has an estimated $60–70 million annualized EBITDA impact. 13 These projections reflect our internal estimates and are subject to change based on company execution and market conditions.

Valuation

At the time of purchase, CENX shares were trading at approximately 6.1x our 2026E EBITDA and roughly 2.1x trailing twelve-month sales. Metals & Mining comps typically trade at 4.0–6.0x EBITDA, but CENX arguably warrants a premium given the structural growth optionality from the Oklahoma mega-project — which at mid-cycle prices could contribute $300–400 million in proportional EBITDA by decade-end. 14 B. Riley recently raised its price target to $86. We believe the market is assigning minimal value to the Oklahoma project and the broader reindustrialization narrative.

Responsible Business

Century's Grundartangi smelter in Iceland runs on nearly 100% renewable energy (geothermal and hydroelectric), producing the company's "Natur-Al" branded low-carbon aluminum for the European market. The Oklahoma project's potential to secure clean energy from wind and solar sources could make it the cleanest large-scale smelter in U.S. history. Century's role in reducing American dependence on imported aluminum from higher-carbon foreign sources provides an additional sustainability dimension. We note that the company disclosed a material weakness in internal controls over financial reporting related to a 2023–2025 restatement, which is being actively remediated. 15

1 SEC Schedule 13D/A (Mar 27, 2026): Glencore plc beneficially owns 29.99% based on 98,969,007 shares outstanding.

data.title: Token Cost Conundrums
data.description:
A couple of weeks ago, I highlighted a couple of pieces by Anjali Shrivastava, who made the point that a token is not a fixed unit of cost. This piece is a follow-up driving the point home that even though every model charges its API customers per token, tokens are far from a standard unit.

Each model has its own tokenizer that decides how many tokens your prompt becomes. Feed the exact same prompt to GPT-5.4 (OPENAI) and Claude Opus 4.7 (ANTHRO), and Claude might slice it into 2–3x as many pieces. So even if the headline price were exactly the same, you'd pay 2–3x more for identical content. It’s like two movers quoting you a rate per box. Mover A quotes you $10 per box but uses large boxes.

On the other hand, Mover B charges you $8 per box, but uses small boxes. Mover B’s rate is 20% cheaper, but your apartment takes 10 boxes at Mover A and 18 at Mover B. As a result, the final bill turns out to be $100 vs. $144; i.e., the “cheaper” quote ends up costing you 44% more. The key thing I want to highlight is Mover B wasn’t being dishonest with you; it was your job to understand the nuances of the quote and choose accordingly.

Instead of seeing texts as letters of words, a language model sees numbers. The tokenizer is the translator sitting at the door: you hand it English or code, and it hands the model back a sequence of numeric IDs. Every model has its own tokenizer with its own vocabulary, typically 50,000 to 250,000 “tokens.” Each token is a chunk of characters that got a dedicated ID. I asked Claude to explain this with an analogy:

“Think of a court stenographer. She has shortcut keystrokes for super common words — “the,” “court,” “objection” — each one keystroke. Uncommon words she has to spell out letter by letter. Her speed on any given trial depends entirely on how well her shortcut system matches the vocabulary of that case. A routine contract dispute flies by. A medical malpractice case full of “anastomosis” and “laparoscopic cholecystectomy” crawls, because those words aren’t in her shortcut set.”

Tokenizers work the same way; they just learn from data. During training, the algorithm scans a huge pile of text and asks, which character sequences show up so often that they deserve their own token? Frequent stuff gets compressed, e.g., “information” is probably one token, maybe two.

However, rare stuff gets shattered, i.e., an obscure chemical name might take eight tokens, one per syllable or letter cluster. Tokenization is essentially a compression algorithm, and compression inherently only works on patterns you’ve seen before.

These all may seem unnecessary details, but after reading a recent piece on TensorZero, I appreciated how these details can have a material impact on how much the customers are paying to the model developers. Some excerpts from TensorZero:

“We sent identical inputs through each provider’s official token counting API and normalized against OpenAI’s:

Multiplying list price by tokenizer efficiency gives you what you actually pay to process the same input.

The differences are dramatic. On tool-heavy workloads, claude-opus-4-7 costs 5.3x more than gpt-5.4 even though their list prices are only 2x apart. The rankings also flip depending on what you’re sending: Gemini (GOOG) (GOOGL) is the cheapest option for text and structured data, but becomes 46% more expensive than OpenAI on tool definitions.

The only way to know what you’re actually paying is to measure it.”

As you can see, tokenization is a non-transparent billing unit that each vendor controls unilaterally. If a provider bills for hidden reasoning or other opaque internal operations, the customer may be paying for a large share of compute they cannot directly observe or verify.

Remember what I said before: the mover wasn’t being dishonest (okay fair, you can probably say they were being clever) to quote you a cheaper price but using a smaller box; it was your job to figure out the difference in box dimension and other details before hiring the mover.

Similarly, after understanding these nuances, I think any enterprise would be really imprudent to standardize on just one model developer. This is because the customer loses bargaining power, a benchmark, and the ability to distinguish real quality differences from billing artifacts.

If the seller controls both the meter and the service, and the buyer has no parallel benchmark, the buyer is highly likely to end up paying more over the long term.

Even if the model developer isn’t sneakily charging you higher price, without any benchmark, how will the customer press the model developer to lower their price or even understand that they’re paying too high a price?

I have gone through a couple of papers on arXiv that laid out these concerns as well. Here’s one paper pointing out the asymmetry of information between the model developers and customers:

“Our key observation is that, in the interaction between a user and a provider, there is an asymmetry of information. The provider observes the entire generative process used by the model to generate an output, including its intermediate steps and the final output tokens, whereas the user only observes and pays for the (output) tokens shared with them by the provider. This asymmetry sets the stage for a situation known in economics as moral hazard, where one party (the provider) has the opportunity to take actions that are not observable by the other party (the user) to maximize their own utility at the expense of the other party.The core of the problem lies in the fact that the tokenization of a string is not unique. For example, consider that the user submits the prompt “What is the oldest city in the world?” to the provider, the provider feeds it into an LLM, and the model generates the output “|Dam|ascus|” consisting of two tokens. Since the user is oblivious to the generative process, a self-serving provider has the capacity to misreport the tokenization of the output to the user without even changing the underlying string. For instance, the provider could simply claim that the LLM generated the tokenization “|D|a|m|a|s|c|u|s|” and overcharge the user for eight tokens instead of two!”

Another paper also highlighted the point that pricing variation can be quite arbitrary:

“We find empirical evidence that, particularly for non-english outputs, both proprietary and open-weights LLMs often generate the same (output) string with multiple different tokenizations, even under the same input prompt, and this in turn leads to arbitrary price variation.”

Even though it may be prudent to not standardize on one model, I do wonder whether the gravitational pull of standardization will be too much to ignore. Enterprises may standardize on a model because they've written prompts against its quirks, built evals around its output style, fine-tuned retrieval to its context window, and trained their engineers on its API.

The cost of rewriting a production AI stack for many enterprise customers may be so high that even if they knew Anthropic's tokenizer was less efficient than GPT's, they might rationally stay with Anthropic. I’ll go back to what Dario Amodei said about why he thinks API business will be much more sticky than some might think:

Dario Amodei:So often I’ll talk about the platform and the importance of the models. For some reason, sometimes people think of the API business and they say, “Oh, it’s not very sticky.” Or, “It’s going to be commoditized.”John Collison:I run an API business. I love API businesses.Dario Amodei:No, no, exactly, exactly. And there are even bigger ones than both of ours. I would point to the clouds again. Those are $100 billion API businesses, and when the cost of capital is high and there are only a few players... And relative to cloud, the thing we make is much more differentiated, right? These models have different personalities, they’re like talking to different people. A joke I often make is, if I’m sitting in a room with ten people, does that mean I’ve been commoditized?John Collison:Yes, yes, yes.Dario Amodei:There’s like nine other people in the room who have a similar brain to me, they’re about the same height, so who needs me? But we all know that human labor doesn’t work that way. And so I feel the same way about this…we’re like one of the biggest customers of the clouds, and we use more than one of them. And I can tell you, the clouds are much less differentiated than the AI models

Nonetheless, the smart move does seem to be multi-model capability (even if 95% of volume goes to one vendor) plus internal benchmarks run on your actual prompts. That gives you the optionality to switch and, more importantly, the negotiating leverage to push back at contract renewal.

Given this context, I believe it will be exceptionally unlikely that enterprise AI will ever be dominated by one model developer. Anthropic may be dominating enterprise AI today, but OpenAI and Google will also likely have plenty of opportunities to gain further ground.

data.description:
Dear Fellow Investor,

Upslope's objective is to deliver attractive, equity-like returns with significantly reduced market risk and low correlation versus traditional equity strategies. Q1 was a period of strong relative and absolute performance for Upslope - albeit with elevated up- and down-side volatility.

Market Conditions - a Strange Game

Early Q1 saw a sharp snapback in relative performance of defensive stocks. Ironically, by the time the war in Iran unfolded, defensives had run so hard that they sharply underperformed in a volatile macro environment typically conducive to good performance. While implications of the war are serious on a human and macroeconomic level, "serious" is not the right word to describe how financial markets processed events. Investors seemed far more interested in "Tweet trading" than any traditional fundamental analysis.

When it comes to navigating big macro events - e.g. wars, pandemics, recessions - Upslope's process is simple. First, determine whether the event is truly material or short-term noise. If the former, the next step is to quickly identify offside positioning in the portfolio. This means reducing outsized exposures to key risks - not eliminating them. The goal isn't to bet on a specific outcome (e.g. quick end to the war or a drawn-out conflict), but to move into balance so that the next breaking development or market-moving Tweet doesn't matter much to the portfolio. While paying close attention to big picture shifts is essential, predicting and acting on daily news flow and associated ups/downs in markets is not Upslope's game. "The only move is not to play."

As is typical during stretches of heightened volatility, Upslope made quite a few adjustments to the portfolio in Q1. Exited positions mostly fell into two categories: theses that had already played out (e.g. Sandvik) and sales aimed at concentrating the portfolio into higher conviction ideas with overlapping exposure (e.g. exiting Japan Exchange while holding CME and MarketAxess). Over time, this approach has evolved into more of a mainstay of Upslope's strategy: purchase a somewhat related group of stocks and narrow them down to fewer, higher-conviction positions over time. More details on these changes and new additions to the portfolio are below.

Portfolio Positioning

As of this writing, gross and beta-adjusted net exposures were 159% and 40%, respectively. Positioning reflects a high number of perceived opportunities on both (long + short) sides of the portfolio. Gross is elevated above the Fund's typical 150% ceiling due to hedged positions in Alphabet (GOOGL) and Intel.

Key Exits

The Fund exited Sandvik (SAND-SE, Sweden-based provider of mining, metal-cutting, and other industrial equipment and tools) and Holcim (HOLN-CH, Switzerland-based, global cement, aggregates and building materials company) due to full valuations and theses generally having played out.

The Fund also exited Japan Exchange Group (8697-JP, largest financial exchange operator in Japan), Booz Allen (BAH) (BAH, defense/security consulting firm), and Bio-Rad (BIO) (BIO, clinical diagnostic and life sciences products). While each situation was unique, the broader theme was one of concentrating the portfolio on other existing ideas and "battles." For Booz, the stock has come under pressure due to AI worries and political risk. While not the only portfolio company exposed to AI risk, Booz was the lowest conviction holding exposed to it and exiting enabled Upslope to add to higher conviction positions (e.g. FTI Consulting).

New Longs

As I've done in the past during unusually active quarters, new longs are presented in a bullet-pointed summary table below.

One supplemental comment: Upslope added three positions that I will awkwardly (because I haven't come up with a better term) refer to as "defensive-plus" businesses. These are diverse, well-managed businesses with large, highly defensive cores complemented by cyclical business units operating in trough-like conditions. This feels like the right market environment to own businesses like these, as they are unusually well positioned to take advantage of macro volatility. Most are also too boring for the current crop of investors to care much about today.

Somewhat by coincidence, each of the three "defensive-plus" additions are headquartered outside the United States (Canada, Sweden, and Finland - apparently Colorado's lack of winter this year is affecting me). In my view, the European additions have loosely similar exposure to the European stocks Upslope exited in Q1 - but with far less demanding valuations.

Exhibit 4: New Long Positions

Description Key Investment Points & Risks
Kesko (KESKOB-FI)

Kesko is the #2 grocer in Finland with nearly 35% market share (contributes 65% of company op profit). It also operates a building products business (26% of op profit) and a smaller Volkswagen-focused auto dealer network. The building products unit is geographically diverse (mostly across the Nordics) and has been acquisitive through the current down-cycle in housing.
  • ✓ Large market share (30-40%+ in key business units) and clean competitive advantages (e.g. unique entrepreneurial operating model) across segments
  • ✓ Operating structure enables it to effectively "fund" more aggressive expansion and consolidation in a challenged economic cycle while competitors retrench (i.e. Grocery unit ballast enables expansion in building products)
  • ✓ Trough-like macro conditions in building products, which could potentially double operating profit in the years ahead
  • ✓ Shares trade near middle of historical valuation range (16x EPS vs. 12-22x) - but on earnings currently depressed in Building and Technical Trade
  • ✗ Competition from discount grocer peers (e.g. Lidl)
  • ✗ Continued/extended macro challenges in cyclical segments
  • ✗ Government regulation (specifically focused on grocer sector)
  • ✗ Acquisition strategy (+ moderate leverage) could falter
Stella-Jones (SJ-CA)

Stella-Jones is a leading N. American manufacturer of specialty wood products for infrastructure. Products are primarily for utility poles (~50% of sales), rail ties (25%), and residential/other (25%). While headquartered in Montreal, 70% of sales are from the US with the remainder from Canada.
  • ✓ Dominant market share in rational/consolidated markets
  • ✓ Steady or better secular demand drivers (e.g. aging grid and infrastructure upgrades)
  • ✓ Strong capital allocation track record - disciplined M&A plus active and aggressive buyback program, while maintaining healthy balance sheet
  • ✓ Attractive valuation (13x EPS vs. 10-20x historically)
  • ✗ Potential for a rise in alternative material (metal) use for utility poles
  • ✗ Further insourcing of rail tie operations
  • ✗ Cyclicity in demand for rail ties, residential/other operations and, to a less correlated extent, utility poles
  • ✗ FX: sizable part of operations and cost base is Canadian
Lifco (LIFCO.B-SE)

Lifco is a Sweden-based serial acquiror diversified across Dental (20% of EBITA), Demolition & Tools (26% - niche machinery and attachments), and Systems Solutions (53% - specialty equipment, systems and manufacturing). The business is geographically diversified, but mostly (82%) focused on Europe.
  • ✓ Serial acquiror with defensive core and diversified cyclical exposure
  • ✓ Shares have de-rated (40x to 30x EPS) and are reasonable considering solid growth (historically 5-10% organic plus 5-10% acquired), strong cash flows/profitability (100% FCF conversion from EPS) and balance sheet (1.0x net leverage)
  • ✓ Cyclical units currently facing macro headwinds and should broadly benefit from eventual resolution of war in Ukraine
  • ✗ Serial acquiror strategy can falter - especially at large scale
  • ✗ Founder influence/control and uncertainty around succession
  • ✗ FX: largely based in Europe
  • ✗ Some overlap/correlation with existing Upslope positions (DPLM-LON + HSIC)
Jack Henry (JKHY)

Based in Missouri, Jack Henry is a leading provider of core processing and payments technology to banks across the U.S. The company is particularly strong and dominant in serving regional banks and credit unions.
  • ✓ Highly defensive, recurring revenue-based (90%) model - products are vital to the operations of JKHY's customers and extremely challenging to swap out
  • ✓ Strong capital allocation record - disciplined/selective M&A, opportunistic buyback and balance sheet that's effectively debt-free today
  • ✓ Sharply de-rated (40x to 20x EPS, 8x to 4x sales) largely due to recent "AI worries" among software businesses following a period of over-valuation
  • ✓ Recent margin inflection appears sustainable; estimates have continued to rise
  • ✗ AI risk - while Upslope's view is AI is more likely a benefit than a headwind, its future impact is impossible to know with certainty (and it will likely be large)
  • ✗ JKHY customer base continues to consolidate
  • ✗ General risks associated with a technology business that touches highly regulated financial services customers/activities (e.g. costly errors, cyber-attacks)

Source: Upslope, FactSet, company filings

Closing Thoughts

Geopolitics-driven volatility seems likely to remain unusually high for the years ahead. While it's impossible to predict the short term, this environment should continue to surface opportunities for those seeking high-quality businesses at attractive valuations. On the short side of the portfolio, actionable ideas abound.

Thank you for the trust you've placed in me and Upslope to manage a portion of your hard-earned money. Please contact me if you have any questions at all, would like to add to your investment, or know a qualified investor who may be a good fit for Upslope's unique approach.

Sincerely,

George K. Livadas

Appendix B: Portfolio Company (Long) Descriptions 4

CME Group (CME) : Leading operator of global derivatives exchanges; focused on rates, commodities, equities and FX. Attractive, durable business that should continue to benefit from global macro volatility. Competitive threat from upstart exchange continues to dissipate.

Crown Holdings (CCK) : One of the leading global aluminum beverage can producers, with strong geographic diversification. Decade-low leverage and EV multiples provide an attractive entry point for a company operating well in an out of favor staples-like sector.

Diploma (DPMAY) : U.K.-based specialty distributor focused on essential consumable products across life sciences, seals (machinery), and controls (aerospace wiring/harnesses). Unique model and conservative M&A strategy have historically enabled attractive free cash flow growth through the cycle.

Fanuc (FANUY), (6954-JP) : Leading global factory automation business exposed to reshoring and physical AI tailwinds. Reasonable valuation and undemanding consensus outlook. Position is highly complementary to Upslope's long and short holdings.

FTI Consulting (FCN) : Boutique consulting and advisory firm, with deep experience in restructuring, dispute/conflict advisory, and other practices. Challenges in a non-core segment have depressed expectations for shares despite an environment that should ultimately lead to strong and rising demand.

Henry Schein (HSIC) : Largest national distributor of dental products, as well as general healthcare supplies. Recent investment from KKR, along with long-time CEO transition, appears likely to accelerate efficiency improvements and revenue growth, while maintaining take-private optionality.

Jack Henry (JKHY) : Niche fintech business focused on core processing and payments, with dominant and entrenched market position among regional banks and credit unions. Steady/accelerating fundamentals in the face of AI headline noise delivered an opportunity to own shares in a growing and defensive company at an attractive price.

MarketAxess (MKTX) : Challenged member of fixed income/credit "exchange" duopoly. Given macro/geopolitical backdrop and depressed valuation, company appears likely to stabilize market share (or better) in the near-term and/or be acquired by a large exchange in the medium-term.

Kesko (KKOYF) (KESKOB-FI) : Uniquely diversified business spanning grocery, building products and auto dealers. Large, defensive grocery business (#2 share in consolidated Finnish market) enables management to allocate capital more aggressively during regional cyclical downturn in building products, as some competitors retrench.

Korea Defense Industry ETF (KDEF) : Concentrated ETF with exposure to South Korea's leading defense and shipbuilding contractors. Beneficiary of elevated/rising geopolitical tensions significant cost advantages vs. US/Eur peers, heightened policy uncertainty, and government-backed pro-shareholder/-market reforms.

Lifco (LFABF) (LIFCO.B-SE) : Storied serial acquiror that has de-rated in the face of various European macro headwinds. Valuation has become reasonable considering the company's track record of solid organic growth, supplemented by consistent and disciplined acquisition growth funded via strong free cash flow.

Smiths Group (SMGZY) (SMIN-LON) : Historically sleepy UK-based industrial conglomerate focused on specialty industrial niches and, previously, security/detection. Activist pressure led management to transform the business by shedding non-core units and focusing on the strongest legacy segments while aggressively returning capital.

Stella-Jones (SJ:CA) (STLJF) (STJNY) : Leading producer of specialty wood products (e.g. utility poles and rail ties) in North America with strong capital allocation track record and secular tailwinds stemming from aging infrastructure. Short-term challenges (e.g. rail tie insourcing) have created an opportunity to own shares at an attractive price.

STERIS (STE) : Leader in infection prevention, sterilization, and procedural solutions for healthcare providers, hospitals, pharmaceutical & bioprocess customers, and medical-device makers. Strong historic organic growth, despite majority of sales from recurring services and consumables.

Teledyne Tech. (TDY) : Producer of specialty electronics, components, and technologies - primarily focused on imaging sensors and cameras. Dinged by transformative 2021 FLIR acquisition, expectations and valuation reset, balance sheet is clean, and the company is positively exposed to rising defense spending and reshoring.

Core Longs : Higher "quality" businesses (defined as low cyclicality, clean balance sheet, obvious and durable competitive advantages) managed with less valuation sensitivity (i.e. typically won't exit a Core long solely because of valuation) and assuming a multi-year time horizon.

Tactical Longs : Traditional "value" investments of more modest quality (reasonably or very cyclical, currently experiencing operational, competitive or financial challenges) managed with greater valuation sensitivity (i.e. typically begin to exit if shares approach full valuation) and assuming a shorter time horizon (typically 6-18 months).

data.title: Synchrony Financial: The Consumer Is Holding On - So Should Investors
data.description:
Q1 Results Show Steady Performance

Synchrony Financial (SYF) reported solid Q1 results, with no signs of consumer stress. Credit quality metrics were steady while purchase volume showed a healthy increase. Earnings outlook for the year was unchanged, however, as loan balances did not grow as much due to consumers paying off their cards more quickly than in prior cycles. The full year EPS guidance remains $9.10 - $9.50, which is also little changed from the 2025 actual result of $9.38.

This justified the downgrade to Hold in my January article. The stock has returned about 5% since then, but better buying opportunities were available during the market correction at the start of Iran hostilities in the first half of March. For the rest of the year, Synchrony expects to get back to growing loan balances due to purchase volume growth and some new card programs. On credit quality, delinquencies are now steadying after a period of declines, but charge-offs are now expected to be below the bottom of prior guidance. These benefits will be partially offset by Synchrony's arrangements with retail partners to share the card program profits. The company is also seeing cost growth mainly from IT expenses even as employee cost increases are minimal. This should pay off in improved efficiency in future years.

Overall, the share price and 2026 outlook have not changed enough to change my Hold rating, but the company's demonstrated success at avoiding consumer credit risk in a volatile environment, expected purchase volume growth, and technology investments driving efficiency all make Synchrony a good long-term investment. The company's continued substantial share count reduction through buybacks is another factor supporting the common shares. Investors desiring more income also have good choices in the preferred shares with either a fixed or 5-year resettable rate available to fit their interest rate outlook.

Consumer Purchasing Remains Solid

Synchrony has not had much cause to talk about the clichéd "K-shaped economy" you hear about so often from politicians, economists, and macro-focused analysts. If I had to assign the economy a letter based on Synchrony's various consumer platforms, I would call it M-shaped. The low and the high end both appear to be growing their spending while the middle remains subdued.

The smaller-ticket items generally purchased in the Diversified & Value and Digital platforms experienced 8-9% year on year purchase volume growth in Q1. The discretionary big-ticket items within the Lifestyle platform, such as motorcycles and other powersports equipment, also had strong purchase volume growth at 7%, although loan receivables growth was lower. These consumers paid off their balances quicker, possibly due to tax refunds in the quarter. It was Home & Auto with flat purchase volume growth and declining loan balances that was the biggest drag on the company in Q1. Still, this segment remains a key focus for new program growth, with the Lowe's (LOW) and Bob's Discount Furniture (BOBS) partnerships I discussed last quarter, along with new deals such as luxury home furnishings seller RH. For the full year, Synchrony is still expecting 5% loan receivables growth compared to the end of 2025.

Credit Quality Improvements Tail Off But Remain Solid

Synchrony's credit quality metrics have steadily improved over the last couple years, but that improvement is starting to flatten out based on 30- and 90-day delinquency rates. Both are almost identical to the rates seen 1 year ago. This is a sign that future improvements in charge-offs and the allowance for credit losses will slow down, but these metrics are currently at healthy levels. The charge-off rate was 5.42% in Q1, nearly 1 percentage point better than a year ago, and the allowance for credit losses was 10.42%, or 45 basis points better than last year.

For the full year, Synchrony is now forecasting a charge-off rate below 5.5%, an improvement from the 5.5-6.0% range projected at the start of this year. Some of this benefit from lower charge-offs is shared with Synchrony's retail partners through RSAs, or Retailer Sharing Arrangements, although RSAs as a percentage of receivables is still forecasted at 4.0-4.5% in 2026.

Operating Expense Growth Is An Investment In Future Efficiency

Operating Expenses grew 6% in Q1, although the biggest component, employee costs, grew just 2%. The big cost driver for the quarter was Information Processing, up 20%. This category includes fees paid to the credit card platforms like Mastercard (MA) but also covers spending on technology to improve the business. On the earnings call, we heard about some uses of AI at the company. On the cost saving side, Synchrony is using AI for coding applications and other functions to keep headcount growth flat. On the revenue enhancement side, Synchrony is working with its retail partners and tech companies to ensure that as agentic AI begins making purchases on behalf of consumers, Synchrony is embedded as a payment option.

For 2026, Synchrony is expecting operating expenses to grow in line with receivables, excluding a one-time $98 million cost last year.

Model Results and Valuation

In addition to the assumptions discussed above, I am revising down my net interest margin expectation for 2026 to 15.5%, in line with the actual Q1 result. This is below my previous estimate of 16.16% due to fewer projected interest rate cuts which would have lowered the cost of deposits. I also slightly increased loss reserves to 10.25% of receivables from 10.1% last quarter.

For 2026, I am forecasting EPS of $9.29, just $0.07 below my estimate last quarter and about a 1% decline from 2025 actuals. At a share price of $78.20, the forward P/E estimate is 8.4, about intermediate between year-end 2024 and 2025 levels.

On the balance sheet, I am assuming buybacks of $900 million per quarter, similar to Q1 levels. Synchrony has changed its buyback policy so that it no longer has an end date. The current buyback authorization recently approved by the board is for $6.5 billion. Still, the company noted on the earnings call they plan to keep buying back shares at a pace similar to recent quarters. It would take about 2 years to work through the current buyback authorization at the current pace.

Synchrony also announced that after a fifth quarterly dividend of $0.30 to be paid in May, they will be raising it to $0.34 starting in August. This 13.3% dividend raise results in a forward yield of about 1.7%.

With this capital return, I am projecting Synchrony to end 2026 with a book value of $48.09 and tangible book value of $39.83. That puts P/B around 1.63 and P/TB at 1.96, cheaper than where it ended 2025 but about in line with year-end 2024 levels.

Synchrony remains well-capitalized as I project year-end common equity / asset ratio to be 11.3%. This is lower than recent years as the bank finally starts growing loan balances again, but it is not too low to be a concern. The CFO mentioned on the earnings call an interest in issuing more preferred shares to increase Tier 1 leverage ratio but did not consider that metric a constraint today.

Looking at the Seeking Alpha Quant Rankings, Synchrony is little changed from last quarter, with a Hold rating and score of 3.16. That places it 19th in the Consumer Finance industry, just under closest peers Ally (ALLY) at 18th and Capital One (COF) at 17th. Synchrony's main shortfall vs. these competitors is in the Growth category with a grade of C-, compared to A for Ally and A- for Capital One. The Growth grade for Synchrony should improve if the company delivers on its plan to increase loan receivables by 5% in 2026. These balances have been flat to down the last couple of years as new program growth has been subdued and customers have been paying off their accounts more quickly than in previous years.

Update on Preferred Shares

Synchrony has two classes of preferred shares. Series A (SYF.PR.A) was issued in 2019 in a lower interest rate environment and pays a fixed coupon rate of 5.625% based on a par value of $25. At a market price of $20.00, the current yield is 7.0%. It is now callable on any payment date, but given the current interest rate environment, a call is highly unlikely. Series B (SYF.PR.B) was issued in 2024 and pays a coupon rate of 8.25% but will reset in 2029 and every 5 years thereafter based on the 5-year US Treasury note yield (US5Y) plus 4.044%. At a market price of $26.20, the current yield is 7.9%. Series B is callable at each reset date. If interest rates are lower in 2029, and the market is comfortable with Synchrony's credit quality, there is some chance of Series B getting called at $25.00, creating a capital loss from the current price. However, I believe this risk is low with Synchrony indicating they are considering issuing more preferred stock. If the 5-year Treasury is still yielding the current rate of 3.93% in 2029, Series B would see a dividend cut to a 7.97% coupon if not called.

Since my last article, Series A has come from behind to slightly outperform Series B, in line with my Buy rating on A and Hold rating on B. The spread of about 90 basis points between the current yields of the two preferreds is similar to last quarter.

The preferred dividends are well-covered. Total preferred dividend payout in 2025 was $83 million, compared to net income before preferred dividends of $3.55 billion. I would not expect the issuance of a new series of preferred shares to materially impact this high coverage.

Note that to avoid a dividend cut in 2029, the 5-year Treasury would have to be yielding more than 4.21% when the rate resets in February 2029. Yields have not exceeded this level since February 2025. The model from econforecasting.com shows the 5-year yield approaching or slightly exceeding this level at some points in the future, but not by much. The risk of a dividend cut for Series B if rates are lower, along with the risk of a call if rates are higher, is a continuing reason to avoid Series B. Series A has the risk of share price decline if rates increase but no risk of a dividend cut. If rates are falling as the Series B reset date approaches in 2029, the price of Series A is much more likely to benefit.

Conclusion

Synchrony common shares are only slightly above where they were trading at the time of my last article when I rated them a Hold, but better prices were available during the March market correction. Synchrony's lending standards, good relationships with retail partners, and use of technology have allowed the bank to continue delivering steady results amid macro worries about the health of the consumer. While earnings growth is still expected to be flat in 2026, the growth in loan balances from new card programs and higher purchase volume sets up the company for EPS growth in future years. EPS growth will also be helped by Synchrony's strong buyback program.

With valuations not expensive but not cheap compared to history, the common shares remain a Hold as the bank has proven itself to be a prudent lender and steward of investors' capital. Any dips into the $60s from general market unease would be buying opportunities.

While Synchrony has favored buybacks over high dividends for returning capital to common shareholders, investors who prefer more income would find Synchrony Series A preferred shares to be a good choice. The preferred dividends are well covered by earnings, including Series B and any likely future preferred issue being considered. Series A would be expected to outperform Series B and avoid being called in an interest rate environment that is flat to declining. Series B remains a Hold due to the risk of a dividend cut or a call at $25 in 2029.

data.title: Groupe Dynamite: Newest Canadian Retail Growth Story
data.description:
Groupe Dynamite (GRGD:CA) is an interesting story with a long history dating back to the 1970s and a recent uptick in popularity. The company is following in the footsteps of its larger competitor Aritzia (ATZ:CA), which has been a darling of the Toronto Stock Exchange for the past 5 years. After its late 2024 IPO, GRGD has shown very impressive growth with accelerating comparable store sales and a growing US presence. The equity has pulled back recently, marking an intriguing entry point for long-term investors looking for something with significant growth potential. At a sub-$10 billion market cap in Canadian dollars, the equity is a small cap, but the company has proven it can grow through a difficult retail cycle with solid long-term management.

Two Emerging Growth Brands - Garage and Dynamite

GRGD is leaning into its two unique retail store brands, Garage and Dynamite, with current US expansion focused on Garage. Garage is focused on the Gen Z late teens to 20s cohort, which is looking for trendy fashion at a reasonable price. Dynamite, focused more on the mid-30s cohort, is seeing repositioning and expansion but not the level of growth being seen at Garage into 2026. Garage is seeing all the new store additions for 2026 in the United States, as it follows in the footsteps of 2025 stalwart Aritzia with everyday luxury-style women's attire. Groupe Dynamite has exceptional margins for a clothing retailer at 60% plus, as they have been increasing prices at over twice the rate of inflation in recent years. This is leading to very strong comparable sales growth and allows management to lean into new store openings and renovations. Garage, in particular, is a hot brand right now, with youth flocking to the reasonably priced casual wear.

After 2019, the company led by Andrew Lufty has been on a more growth-focused trajectory, which you can see from the improving same-store sales below. Improving data on the customer base and a more professional management team have led the company to significantly improve the financial performance. This inflected in fiscal 2025 with strong margin expansion and same-store sales growth of 26.7% in the full year. Stores are paying back in less than two years, and the time to market for GRGD products is giving them a structural advantage. They are purchasing much of their product in-season, allowing them to capitalize on trends more than other brands. This also enables them to maximize their margins and reduce markdowns to a low level as they reduce product that is out of style for each season. This has proven very successful for them, and the power of US growth is now showing through strongly as well. The company still has significant untapped potential through both US store openings as well as international expansion. They have opened a store in London and will use that to learn about the European market for coming expansion. At a limited number of stores, GRGD has significant gains to be made in both awareness and spreading of their retail locations across the US as well.

The fiscal fourth quarter, just reported on April 1st, was very strong, with comparable sales staying at an exceptional rate for the second quarter in a row at a stellar 30.4%. This led to 40.3% revenue growth in the quarter, with 43.3% growth in online sales. Online sales are a very small portion of sales at just 17.4% of total retail sales. Look for this number to grow faster than the store business and become a greater portion over time, helping to boost growth. Gross margins came in at 66.1% - a level higher than the most profitable clothing companies out there - showing strong purchasing and inventory discipline. This led to a phenomenal increase in earnings of 75% year over year, as operating income was up 90% y/y to $120.3 million. At a 33% operating margin, the company has impressive structural margins considering the currently difficult operating environment most of its peers are facing. The company only has $183m left of net debt, leading to a net cash position within a year. This will allow the company to continue to redeploy cash to shareholders through buybacks or accelerate growth plans for international expansion. While the company guided to 11-14% comparable sales growth for the year, this number is likely to be revised higher with good execution. A number above 15% would support a continued earnings multiple in the high 20% to low 30% range, depending on operating margin. The 35x earnings multiple it reached in December will be difficult to reach with growth slowing from here due to difficult comparisons.

The unique inventory management capabilities have propelled the US market growth with 131 stores in the US. They are adding a large amount of new Garage stores in the US, with Dynamite focused predominantly in Canada. They are focused on high-end locations and plan to actually close some of the weaker locations to upscale the offering. Above you can see that while the most recent year of same-store sales growth may decelerate to a more reasonable 10%, the company has solid upside from there. The US market continues to prove lucrative, allowing for share improvement even with high expectations. Below you can see that shares are off from the highs to a reasonable 29x forward earnings. Growth will be slower in 2026 than in 2025, but operating margins for the full year will be higher as scale continues to increase. Estimates are starting at a higher point than last year but still have good potential to move up from the 22% level currently closer to 30%.

On April 20, CEO Andrew Lufty disclosed he was selling $251 million in shares through his holding company, reducing his stake several percent. However, he still owns 81.9% of the shares, making him extremely aligned with shareholders for long-term value creation. These continued sales that will happen from time to time will actually help GRGD become more accessible for money managers and funds, as it has very low liquidity as of now. Long term I see this as a positive, but it has put shares under pressure with an 11% drop on April 21st due to the optics of the transaction and increase in float size. This is a buying opportunity that I will use to add to my existing positions, as it has no impact on the long-term fundamental trajectory of the business.

Further 2026 Upside is Coming

GRGD has a long runway to grow, with the new UK stores just starting to contribute and a modest foothold in the US with Garage. The plan is for a slow and measured move into Europe, increasing profitability and growth with minimal costs. I see forward multiple remaining in the high 20s to low 30s depending on the next few quarters, supporting another strong 2026 result. Estimates are continuing to improve as growth stays underappreciated with their nimble operating model. The earnings multiple will likely not expand much higher beyond 30 as growth slows, but steady growth and operating margin expansion should still have GRGD shares significantly outperforming the wider market in 2026.
